content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of size NNN.

You can rearrange the elements of AAA as you want. After that, construct an array BBB of size NNN as:


B1=A1B_1 = A_1B1​=A1​;
Bi=Bi−1⊕AiB_i = B_{i - 1} \oplus A_iBi​=Bi−1​⊕Ai​ for all 2≤i≤N2 \le i \le N2≤i≤N, where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operator.
Find a rearrangement of AAA such that Bi≠0B_i \ne 0Bi​=0 for all 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN, the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers : A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers denoting the rearrangement of AAA satisfying the conditions.

In case no such rearrangement exists, print −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 2·10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line. If a rearrangement of the given array exists such that every prefix XOR is non‑zero, print N space‑separated integers – the required permutation. If no such permutation exists, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: total XOR = 1⊕2⊕3 = 0, so impossible.
Test case 2: after sorting and fixing the zero prefix we obtain [1,2,4,3,5]; all prefix XORs are non‑zero.
Test case 3: total XOR = 1⊕2⊕2 = 1 ≠ 0, the sorted order already works.
Test case 4: single element 1 is non‑zero, so the answer is [1].

solution.approach:
Let pref_i be the XOR of the first i elements of the chosen order. We need pref_i ≠ 0 for every i. The XOR of the whole array (total) equals pref_N. If total = 0, the last prefix would be zero for any order, therefore the answer is impossible. If total ≠ 0 we can always construct a valid order.

Greedy construction:
1. Sort the array in non‑decreasing order.
2. Scan the array from left to right while maintaining the current prefix XOR.
3. If after adding A[i] the prefix becomes zero, swap A[i] with A[i+1] (i < N). Because total ≠ 0, there is at least one element to the right that differs in the most significant set bit, guaranteeing the swap makes the prefix non‑zero.
4. Continue the scan. When the scan finishes, no prefix is zero.

The algorithm works because a zero prefix can only appear when the current prefix XOR equals the next element. Swapping with a later element changes the most significant bit of the prefix, preventing zero. The final element cannot make the prefix zero unless total = 0, which we have already excluded.

Edge cases handled:
- N = 1: possible only if the single element is non‑zero.
- Presence of zeros in the array: a zero can be placed after any non‑zero prefix without creating a zero prefix.
- Total XOR = 0: answer is -1.

Complexities: sorting O(N log N) per test case, scanning O(N). Overall O(Σ N log N) ≤ O(2·10^5 log 2·10^5). Memory O(N).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting)
